senses.definition: A person who does detailed and careful study to discover new facts or knowledge, especially in a university or laboratory.
examples: A skilled researchist designed the experiment to test the new drug.
She works as a medical researchist at a large teaching hospital.

senses.definition: Plural of researchist: people who do detailed and careful study to discover new facts or knowledge, especially in universities or laboratories.
examples: Many researchists collaborated on the international climate study.
The conference brought together researchists from over thirty countries.

senses.definition: To add more salt, spices, or flavorings to food again to improve its taste.
examples: Taste the soup and reseason it if it is too bland.
The chef decided to reseason the stew before serving it to the guests.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of reseason: added more salt, spices, or flavorings to food again to improve its taste.
examples: She reseasoned the sauce with a little more salt and pepper.
They tasted the stew, then reseasoned it with fresh herbs.

senses.definition: Describes food that has had extra salt, spices, or flavorings added again.
examples: The reseasoned soup now had a richer, deeper flavor.
We served the chicken with a reseasoned gravy.

senses.definition: Present participle of reseason: adding more salt, spices, or flavorings to food again to improve its taste.
examples: He is reseasoning the curry because it turned out too mild.
After simmering, try reseasoning the soup before you serve it.

senses.definition: The act of adding more salt, spices, or flavorings to food again.
examples: A quick reseasoning can save a dish that tastes flat.
The recipe suggests a final reseasoning just before serving.

senses.definition: Third person singular of reseason: adds more salt, spices, or flavorings to food again to improve its taste.
examples: If the soup tastes too weak, she reseasons it with more herbs.
He always reseasons the sauce before it goes to the table.

senses.definition: To put someone in a different seat, or to show them to a new place to sit.
examples: The usher had to reseat the guests after the mistake in the ticket numbers.
The manager reseated us at a quieter table near the window.

senses.definition: To put a part back firmly into its correct position, especially a mechanical or electronic part.
examples: Try reseating the cable if the monitor is not working.
The technician reseated the battery to fix the connection problem.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of reseat: put someone in a different seat or showed them to a new place to sit.
examples: The flight attendant reseated the couple in an exit row.
Because of the leak, the staff reseated everyone in another section of the restaurant.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of reseat: put a part back firmly into its correct position.
examples: The engineer reseated the loose connector on the motherboard.
He reseated the valve to stop the small leak.

senses.definition: Present participle of reseat: putting someone in a different seat or showing them to a new place to sit.
examples: The staff are reseating customers because of a water leak in the back room.
They are reseating passengers whose seats were double-booked.

senses.definition: Present participle of reseat: putting a part back firmly into its correct position.
examples: Try reseating the memory modules to solve the boot problem.
The mechanic is reseating the gasket to prevent future leaks.

senses.definition: The act of giving people new seats or moving a part back into its correct position.
examples: The reseating of the audience took several minutes after the alarm.
A quick reseating of the cable fixed the picture on the screen.

senses.definition: Third person singular of reseat: puts someone in a different seat or shows them to a new place to sit.
examples: When there is a problem, the usher reseats the guests without complaint.
The system automatically reseats passengers when the plane is changed.

senses.definition: Third person singular of reseat: puts a part back firmly into its correct position.
examples: If the device fails, the technician reseats the card and tests it again.
The mechanic reseats the valve whenever he replaces the seal.

senses.definition: A fine net-like background or foundation in lace.
examples: The antique lace had a delicate reseau that was difficult to repair.
This pattern is worked over a simple reseau of twisted threads.

senses.definition: A grid of fine lines placed on a photographic plate to help measure the positions of stars or other objects.
examples: The astronomer used a reseau to measure the distance between the star images.
By comparing the reseau lines with the star field, they calculated the exact coordinates.

senses.definition: A fine pattern of colored lines or dots used in some types of color photography or printing.
examples: Old color plates show a visible reseau when you look closely.
The reseau on the screen helps create the final color image.

senses.definition: Plural of reseau: net-like backgrounds in lace, or grids used in photography and astronomy.
examples: Different reseaus are used for different astronomical instruments.
The museum displayed several laces with finely worked reseaus.

senses.definition: Plural of reseau, used especially in technical or French-influenced writing.
examples: The photographic plates were marked with finely etched reseaux.
She studied the different reseaux found in historical lace pieces.

senses.definition: To cut out or remove part of an organ or structure surgically.
examples: The surgeon decided to resecate a small section of the damaged intestine.
If the tumor is localized, doctors may be able to resecate it completely.

senses.definition: To secrete something again, such as a fluid or substance produced by a cell or organ.
examples: Under certain conditions, the glands may resecrete the stored hormone.
The cell can resecrete proteins that were taken back inside.

senses.definition: The act or process of secreting a substance again.
examples: The resecretion of the hormone helps the body adjust to stress.
This drug can trigger the resecretion of stored insulin.

senses.definition: To remove part or all of an organ or structure by surgery.
examples: The team decided to resect the diseased portion of the colon.
If possible, surgeons try to resect the tumor with a clear margin.

senses.definition: Plural of resectability: different situations or cases showing how possible it is to remove a tumor or tissue completely by surgery.
examples: The study compared the resectabilities of various liver tumors under new guidelines.
Different resectabilities were observed depending on the tumor’s location in the lung.

senses.definition: The possibility of completely removing a tumor or tissue by surgery.
examples: Tumor size and location strongly influence its resectability.
Chemotherapy may improve the resectability of certain cancers.

senses.definition: Able to be removed completely by surgery.
examples: The tumor is small and appears to be fully resectable.
In some patients, liver metastases are resectable after chemotherapy.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of resect: removed part or all of an organ or structure by surgery.
examples: The surgeon resected the diseased lung tissue.
They successfully resected the tumor with clear margins.

senses.definition: Describes tissue or a tumor that has been removed by surgery.
examples: The pathologist examined the resected specimen under the microscope.
Analysis of the resected tumor confirmed the diagnosis.

senses.definition: Present participle of resect: removing part or all of an organ or structure by surgery.
examples: The team is resecting a portion of the small intestine.
Resecting the tumor completely may improve the patient’s chances.

senses.definition: Describes a procedure or action that involves cutting out part or all of an organ.
examples: A resecting procedure was chosen instead of a transplant.
Resecting surgery can be very demanding for the patient.

senses.definition: A surgical operation in which part or all of an organ or structure is cut out.
examples: The patient underwent a bowel resection to remove the diseased area.
Lung resection may be necessary for certain types of cancer.

senses.definition: Relating to or involving the surgical cutting out of part of an organ or structure.
examples: The surgeons chose a resectional approach rather than a transplant.
Resectional techniques have improved survival rates for some cancers.

senses.definition: Plural of resection: surgical operations in which parts or whole organs are cut out.
examples: Colon and liver resections are common procedures in cancer treatment.
The hospital performs many lung resections each year.

senses.definition: A thin medical instrument that is put into the body to remove tissue, often used in surgery through natural openings, such as the bladder.
examples: The surgeon used a resectoscope to remove the enlarged prostate tissue.
Modern resectoscopes allow doctors to operate with smaller cuts.

senses.definition: Third person singular of resect: removes part or all of an organ or structure by surgery.
examples: The surgeon usually resects the affected portion of the bowel.
If possible, the team resects the tumor in one piece.

senses.definition: To make something safe, fixed, or held in place again.
examples: After the storm, workers had to resecure the loose roof panels.
Please resecure the straps before you start driving again.

senses.definition: To get or obtain something again, especially something that was lost or at risk.
examples: The company worked hard to resecure its main contract.
They are trying to resecure their position in the market.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of resecure: made something safe, fixed, or held in place again.
examples: They resecured the ladder to prevent it from slipping.
The mechanic resecured the loose cables under the hood.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of resecure: got or obtained something again.
examples: The firm resecured its contract after new negotiations.
They resecured funding from their main investor.

senses.definition: Third person singular of resecure: makes something safe, fixed, or held in place again.
examples: He first checks and then resecures the harness before each climb.
The technician resecures the cover after adjusting the settings.

senses.definition: Third person singular of resecure: gets or obtains something again.
examples: The company resecures its license every five years.
She resecures her place on the team with consistent performance.

senses.definition: Present participle of resecure: making something safe, fixed, or held in place again.
examples: He is resecuring the shelves after noticing they were loose.
Resecuring the straps will help prevent accidents during transport.

senses.definition: Present participle of resecure: getting or obtaining something again.
examples: The team is resecuring funding for the next phase of the project.
Resecuring their market share will take time and effort.

senses.definition: A plant of the Reseda genus, such as mignonette, with many small flowers in dense clusters.
examples: The garden border was filled with reseda and other fragrant flowers.
Reseda grows well in sunny, well-drained soil.

senses.definition: A grayish or yellowish green color named after the reseda plant.
examples: She painted the walls a soft reseda to create a calm atmosphere.
The designer chose reseda curtains to match the natural theme.

senses.definition: A family of flowering plants that includes the genus Reseda.
examples: Resedaceae is a small family mostly found in Mediterranean regions.
Plants in Resedaceae are often grown for their fragrance.

senses.definition: Belonging to or resembling the plant family Resedaceae.
examples: The resedaceous plants in this area are known for their scent.
This species has resedaceous features in its flowers.

senses.definition: Plural of reseda: plants of the Reseda genus, often grown for their small, fragrant flowers.
examples: She planted resedas along the path to add color and scent.
Resedas and other low flowers bordered the lawn.

senses.definition: To see someone or something again.
examples: I hope to resee my friends when I visit next year.
Watching the movie a second time helped me resee details I had missed.

senses.definition: To plant seeds again on land, especially to thicken grass or replace plants that have died.
examples: We need to reseed the lawn where the grass has worn away.
Farmers often reseed fields after a poor harvest.

senses.definition: To spread seeds naturally again, for example when plants drop seeds by themselves.
examples: Wildflowers reseed themselves easily in this climate.
If you leave some plants uncut, they will reseed the area.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of reseed: planted seeds again on land.
examples: They reseeded the damaged lawn in early spring.
The field was reseeded after the flood destroyed the crops.

senses.definition: Present participle of reseed: planting seeds again on land.
examples: We are reseeding the lawn this weekend.
Reseeding the pasture will improve the quality of the grass.

senses.definition: The act of planting seeds again on land.
examples: Autumn is a good time for reseeding of lawns.
The government supports the reseeding of damaged forests.

senses.definition: Third person singular of reseed: plants seeds again on land.
examples: Each year he reseeds the thin areas of his lawn.
The farmer reseeds the field after harvesting.

senses.definition: Third person singular of reseed: spreads seeds naturally again.
examples: This flower reseeds easily and can spread across the whole garden.
If left alone, the plant reseeds itself each year.

senses.definition: Present participle of resee: seeing someone or something again.
examples: He looked forward to reseeing his hometown after many years.
Reseeing the film helped her understand the story better.

senses.definition: The act of seeing something again, often with a new understanding.
examples: A second reading can offer a reseeing of the text.
The exhibition invites a reseeing of familiar images.

senses.definition: To seek or look for something again.
examples: They had to reseek answers after the first study failed.
You may need to reseek help if your symptoms return.

senses.definition: Present participle of reseek: seeking or looking for something again.
examples: The committee is reseeking proposals after rejecting the first round.
Reseeking support will be necessary after the policy change.

senses.definition: Third person singular of reseek: seeks or looks for something again.
examples: If the application fails, she reseeks permission from the authorities.
The company reseeks funding when the initial offer falls through.

senses.definition: Past participle of resee: seen again.
examples: The film, when reseen years later, felt completely different.
Old places, once reseen, can bring back strong memories.

senses.definition: Third person singular of resee: sees someone or something again.
examples: Each time he resees the painting, he notices something new.
She resees her doctor every six months for a check‑up.

senses.definition: To divide something into different parts or segments again, often in a new way.
examples: The company plans to resegment the market by age group.
You may need to resegment the data to see clearer patterns.

senses.definition: The act of dividing something into different segments again, often using new criteria.
examples: Market resegmentation led to a more focused advertising strategy.
Resegmentation of the data revealed several new customer groups.

senses.definition: To separate people or things again into groups, especially in a way that keeps different races or classes apart.
examples: Critics argued that the new policy would resegregate the school system.
We must avoid laws that effectively resegregate communities.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of resegregate: separated people or things again into groups, especially by race or class.
examples: Over time, housing patterns resegregated many urban neighborhoods.
Some districts have become resegregated despite earlier integration efforts.
